title: Sonoma Wines Have Never Been This Excellent. Here’s What to Drink Right Now
summary:
While Sonoma may be among California’s most prolific wine regions, what really stands out is its range. These top-rated bottles prove it.

The post Sonoma Wines Have Never Been This Excellent. Here’s What to Drink Right Now appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Sonoma County is large. Very large. The region encompasses over a million total acres, stretching from the Pacific Ocean inland to the Mayacamas Range. It houses some sixty thousand acres of vines and over four hundred wineries. While Sonoma may be among the Golden State’s most prolific wine regions, what really stands out is its range. 

Chardonnay and Pinot Noir can be rich and opulent, complex and high-toned, and everything in between. Bordeaux varieties can be examples of pure power or charming nuance. Rhône varieties? Cal-Itals? German and Austrian varieties? You name it, someone in Sonoma has it planted. 

While Sonoma may be best known for Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, it pays to get off the beaten path and try something new. Recent vintages in this diverse area have produced a tremendous crop of exceptional wines. It’s well worth taking a moment to examine what’s new in the region.

Weather Report

It’s hard to make blanket statements about the weather in a region as varied as Sonoma. Hot inland weather might be contrasted by fog in hillier, more coastal zones. Windswept ridges closer to the Pacific shield valleys and trap warm air. The Petaluma Gap opens to the ocean, allowing huge swaths of Sonoma Valley to “breathe.” 

Further complicating the climate is the fact that weather has become increasingly unpredictable. In 2022, a hot summer and oppressive heat dome around Labor Day forced many growers to make snap decisions around harvest time. The best of these wines are opulent but refined, embracing the decadence of the vintage. 

By way of contrast, 2023 was cooler and wetter than usual. Producers who favor long hangtime and gradual flavor development loved seeing later harvest dates. These wines bring a backbone of notable acidity and the best versions display extreme complexity. The recently released 2024s that are just hitting the market show a more “in-between” profile. Not too hot, not too cold, not too wet. Great examples show nuance and ripeness in tandem… a bit of a Goldilocks situation from a climate perspective.

Trend Report

One primary trend informs much of the innovation happening among Sonoma County’s winemakers in recent years: the gradual sway away from overly oaked and potently alcoholic styles. Instead of harvesting exclusively by sugar levels, producers in Sonoma are looking at acids, phenolic ripeness, and other markers to determine pick dates. The resulting wines possess greater balance, acidity, and freshness. 

Everywhere you look, you find improved farming methods. Sustainable, organic, regenerative, dry farming, and biodynamic vineyard practices are all part of the conversation. Low and no-till rows are becoming far more common, and permanent cover crops are increasing soil and vine health. When winemakers pair these more holistic vineyard practices with experimental varieties, plus the increased use of concrete, amphora, and larger format vessels, you see the picture of a region that embraces its history as a California standard bearer, but one that lets the site control the narrative.  

What to Drink Right Now

Here are some recently reviewed Sonoma wines to suit everyone’s taste, from your steak-loving uncle to the too-cool-for-school twenty-something in your life.

Aromatic Whites

Morning fog and cool nighttime temperatures help keep acids high in Sonoma County. That’s perfect for crisp, refreshing whites. Sauvignon Blanc certainly thrives here in an array of styles, but examples of White Pinot Noir, Vermentino, and many more are also worth seeking out.

Shared Notes 2024 Les leçons des maîtres Sauvignon Blanc (Russian River Valley)

This plush and seductive Sauvignon Blanc shows vibrant aromas of tart pineapple, apricot, ginger, lime, and orange blossom on the nose, while the opulent palate delivers flavors of ripe Meyer lemon, lime, lemongrass and pineapple sage through a long finish. There’s enough structure and tension here for a few years in the cellar. Cellar Selection. 94 points — Tom R. Capo

$95 JJ Buckley

Emeritus Vineyards 2024 Hallberg Blanc Pinot Noir (Russian River Valley)

The nose of this dry farmed White Pinot Noir brings aromas of Golden Delicious apple, candied ginger, lemon blossoms, and pineapple sage. The palate vibrates with energy and juicy acidity, singing with flavors of roasted pineapple, tart nectarine, and Ranier cherry that sit in a framework of pithy texture and bright florals through the finish. Editors’ Choice. 94 points — TRC

$45 Emeritus Vineyards

Reeve 2023 Vermentino (Sonoma Coast)

A big, beautiful nose of white flowers, lime peel and river rocks bursts from the glass of this Vermentino. The palate is focused and nervy, with loads of citrus, finishing with a wave of salty Meyer lemon that’s just like summer in a glass. 93 Points — TRC

$31 Wine.com

Chardonnay

From the Russian River Valley to the Petaluma Gap and up the hillsides of the Mayacamas, Sonoma is Chardonnay country. Some of California’s most famous examples come from these areas. Chardonnay is a chameleon, showing dazzling acidity in some expressions and rich structure in others. Sonoma County is capable of producing almost any version you can imagine.

Knights Bridge 2023 Kelley Chardonnay (Knights Valley)

A whisper of flinty reduction mingles with aromas of tangerine, caramelized pineapple, and white flowers on the nose of this Chardonnay. The palate is energetically acidic, with flavors of green apple, juicy lemon, and ginger, with pithy citrusy acid driving the finish. Editors’ Choice. 95 points — TRC

$110 Knights Bridge

Sangiacomo Wines 2023 Four Siblings Chardonnay (Sonoma Coast)

This sumptuous native yeast and concrete fermented Chardonnay delivers an explosive nose of nectarine, pineapple and white flower aromas. The palate delivers flavors of concentrated apple and stony minerality with biscuity notes from sur lie aging, energetic acidity and salty, pithy Meyer lemon lingering on the finish. 97 points — TRC

$70 Sangiacomo Wines

Croix 2023 Richard Dinner Chardonnay (Sonoma Mountain)

Absolutely gorgeous aromas of ripe Gala apple, ginger, crème brulee and pineapple sage swirl on the nose of this generous native fermented Chardonnay. The palate delivers plush, almost sensual texture, vibrant acidity and concentrated flavors of nectarine, cinnamon and caramel that intensify and soften in waves along the finish. Cellar Selection. 97 points — TRC

Inquire with winery

Pinot Noir

The Russian River Valley is one of the world’s best-known regions for Pinot, but areas like Petaluma Gap and West Sonoma Coast are carving out a reputation for consistently exceptional quality. Perhaps most interestingly, growers working in cooler zones within the Russian River Valley are crafting more aromatically intense and high-toned examples.

Blue Farm 2022 Gap’s Crown Vineyard Pinot Noir (Petaluma Gap)

Baked cherry, spiced berry coulis and cocoa powder aromas jump from the glass on the nose of this complex and rich Pinot. The palate brings concentrated cherry, cinnamon and fried shiitake flavors in a framework of soft tannin and raspberry lemon acidity. Best from 2028-2040. Cellar Selection. 95 points — TRC

Inquire with winery

Williams Selyem 2023 Terra de Promissio Vineyard Pinot Noir (Sonoma Coast)

Aromas of baked cherry, cranberry, nori, cedar and roses waft from the glass on the nose of this Pinot. The palate gives more concentrated berry character, with loads of floral energy and rich tannin that will soften with a little time in the cellar. Best from 2030-2045. Cellar Selection. 96 points — TRC

$110 Total Wine

Senses 2023 Bodega Thieriot Vineyard Pinot Noir (West Sonoma Coast)

This earthy and savory native fermented Pinot displays aromas of tart cranberry, nori, shiitake and plum skin on the nose, giving way to flavors of tart cherry, fried mushrooms, sandalwood and sage on the palate, with fresh orange peel acidity and echoes of clove along the finish. Best from 2030-2040. Cellar Selection. 97 points — TRC

Inquire with winery

Kanzler Vineyards 2023 One Fifteen Pinot Noir (Russian River Valley)

The nose of this Pinot leads with aromas of crushed black cherries, dark cocoa powder, violets and black tea, giving way to flavors of tart raspberry, cinnamon, nori and fried shiitake on the palate, with bouncy acidity and just a bit of grippy tannin at the end. Editors’ Choice. 95 points — TRC

Inquire with winery

Cabernet Sauvignon

Once thought of as Napa’s little cousin, Sonoma Cabernet Sauvignon is becoming a major player globally, with top examples rivaling the great wines of the world. Moon Mountain, Rockpile, and other high-elevation destinations are creating wines of substance, nuance, and character.

Far Mountain 2022 Montecillo Cabernet Sauvignon (Moon Mountain District Sonoma County)

Aromas of black plum, licorice, cracked pepper and flowers drive the nose of this Cabernet from a historic high elevation vineyard. The palate is layered with flavors of dusty cherry, cocoa powder, spiced plum compote, fresh flowers, and graphite that linger on and on and on. This needs time to unfold properly, best from 2030 on. Cellar Selection. 96 points — TRC

$100 K&L Wines

The Setting Wines 2022 Rockpile Ridge Vineyard Cabernet (Rockpile)

Bright, focused aromas of concentrated raspberry, mint and iron-laden earth erupt from the glass on the nose of this powerful Cabernet. The palate is sumptuous, giving velvety tannins, bright acidity and flavors of berry coulis, black plum, herb-spice mélange and violets that linger along the finish. Delicious now, this will reward patience. Best from 2030-2045. Cellar Selection. 98 points — TRC

Inquire with winery

Judge Palmer 2022 Monte Rosso Vineyard Cabernet Sauvignon (Moon Mountain District Sonoma County)

The nose of this complex and enticing Cabernet gives aromas of dusty cherry, spiced plum compote, pencil shavings, cocoa, and flowers. The palate brings potent tannins balanced with mouthwatering acidity and flavors of blackberry gastrique, currants, cinnamon, and chopped mint leaves along with a compelling minute-long finish. Give this time to unfold in the cellar and it will become a showstopper. Cellar Selection. 95 points — TRC

$95 Emmitt-Scorsone

Something New

In a region with so much diversity of soil and climate, there’s a hillside for almost any variety. Thoughtful experiments like this amphora-aged Viognier, Carbonic Grenache, and seldom-planted Austrian red are well worth seeking out.

Jacuzzi 2024 Clarum White Blend (Petaluma Gap)

This amber-tinged, amphorae aged blend erupts from the glass with aromas of concentrated peach, lemongrass, Meyer lemon, and white flowers. The palate is decadent, with rich extraction, plush acidity, and flavors of green apple, lemon peel, jasmine, and tangerine thrumming through the finish. Editors’ Choice. 94 points — TRC

$45 Jacuzzi Wines

Smith Story 2024 BRAVE Grenache (Dry Creek Valley)

This carbonic Grenache is positively joyful, with bright strawberry, rose petal, and raspberry aromas exploding from the glass on the nose. The palate vibrates with crunchy tannins, juicy acidity and loads of berry and floral character. Perfect with a little chill and some chill tunes. 94 points — TRC

$40 Smith Story Wines

Ottavino 2023 Ricci Vineyard St. Laurent (Los Carneros)

Aromas of concentrated black cherry, bacon, violets and pencil lead drive the nose of this native fermented St. Laurent. The palate brings nervy acidity and balanced tannins, with flavors of sour cherry, fresh flowers and herbs mingling before a plushly textured finish. 93 points. 93 points — TRC

title: Vinho Verde Is the Crisp Summer Wine You’ve Been Looking For
summary:
No, Vinho Verde isn't actually green. Here's everything you need to know about the Portuguese region producing top-rated bottles.

The post Vinho Verde Is the Crisp Summer Wine You’ve Been Looking For appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
If there’s one thing to know about Vinho Verde—which in Portuguese translates to “green wine”—it’s that this wine isn’t green. While it’s a common misconception, Vinho Verdo simply refers to bottlings from a region in Portugal of the same name. (That name likely originated because wines from this area were historically released young.)

With tart acidity and a subtle fizz, these wines are perfect for sipping in the summer sun. Indeed, after getting to know them, you might make Vinho Verde your go-to wine region for warm weather. Here’s everything you need to know before picking up your first bottle.

What Is Vinho Verde?

Vinho Verde is a wine region wedged into the northwest corner of Portugal, just below the border with Spain. Similar to many other areas like Bordeaux or Rioja, these wines are often sold under the region name. In addition to its devilish green shadow, another common misconception about Vinho Verde is that the region only produces white wines, but the range of wines is actually just as wide as any other region.

“You can make white, red, rosé, vinegar, brandy, or sparkling wine as long as it’s made with autochthonous varieties,” says Tiago Soares, winemaker at Sapateiro winery in Vinho Verde. “If they are grown inside the region and have a spectrum of flavors that are expected for these wines, that’s Vinho Verde.”

But it hasn’t always been this way. Up until the ‘80s, most of the wines produced here were red because of local preference. However, he explains, once Portugal began exporting more wines, they discovered that the region’s style of light, acidic wine is better served by white varietals, and whites became more prominent.

“Most people associate Vinho Verde with a style of white wine which may be described as light, fresh, aromatic, and slightly [effervescent],” explains João Miguel Maia, producer of Portugal-based Casa de Vilacetinho winery. But these tasting notes can vary depending on the sub-region and the winemaker. Citrus, ranging from lime to lemon zest to grapefruit, is common, but some styles are more ripe with stonefruit and tropical flavors.

Regardless of color or flavors, all Vinho Verde wines have high acidity and relatively low alcohol. However, “the biggest differentiating feature of Vinho Verde wines is that they are lightly sparkling,” says Miguel Maia. “In the past, this fizziness was obtained through a natural second fermentation in the bottle, similar to a pét-nat.” Nowadays, winemakers often add carbon dioxide at the bottling stage to achieve the spritz-like texture.

So, Where Did the Name ‘Vinho Verde’ Come From?

“Some people think that the grapes are harvested early, or harvested when they’re green and therefore they make a green wine,” says Soares. “But it’s not necessarily because of that.” Nor is it because the region is verdant all year round, he adds.

The name likely comes from the fact that the wines were traditionally released young—within three to six months after harvest, Soares says. This lent them a fresh and light characteristic with high acidity and low alcohol. These days, Vinho Verde wines are still released at a young age, so the fresh qualities remain prominent today.

How and When to Drink Vinho Verde

“Vinho Verde are wines that should be consumed immediately due to their light flavor profile and delicate effervescence,” Anna-Christina Cabrales, Wine Enthusiast’s tasting director, says. “You don’t want the bottles to be too old, as they may fall flat or not feel as energetic.” 

With their light and fresh profile, Vinho Verde wines lend themselves particularly well to summertime settings and summery foods. Soares suggests pairing with spicy foods, as the wine’s fresh fizz acts as the perfect palate cleanser. Miguel Maia enjoys Vinho Verde alongside summery seafoods and salads, but he notes that they are equally delicious all on their own. Regardless of the time, place, or pairing, make sure to serve these wines with a good chill.

When it comes to Vinho Verde, your best bet is to hit your local bottle shop to pick up the freshest release. Nevertheless, we’ve pulled together some of our recent favorites to give you an idea of what to look for. Next time you’re in the market for a bright and zesty wine, keep these top-rated Vinho Verdes in mind. 

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Soalheiro 2024 Primeiras Vinhas Alvarinho (Vinho Verde Monção e Melgaço)

This wine, with its tropical fruit aroma, is rich and finely textured. It has a hazelnut and citrus flavor that gives the wine both freshness and ripeness. Drink the wine from 2026. Suitable for vegans. Editors’ Choice. 92 Points — Roger Voss

$28 Wine.com

Aveleda 2024 Solos de Xisto Alvarinho (Vinho Verde)

There’s a distinct gracefulness to this bottling, as genteel mixed citrus, apples, and melon rind lay a foundation for white flowers, white pepper, and wet rock on the nose. The underlying mineral note anchors the wine and lends a little gravitas. Calls out for sushi and will be excellent over the next 2-3 years. Best Buy. 91 Points — John Sumners

$23 Wine.com

Quinta da Pedra 2022 Graça da Pedra Alvarinho (Vinho Verde)

After a few years, Alvarinho deepens and matures well. That’s the case with this wine. It has a ripe lemon aroma that leads to a wine with richness and a touch of aging. It is ready to drink. Best Buy. 91 Points — R.V.

$17 MacArthur Beverages

Quinta de Santa Teresa 2022 Singular Portuguese White (Vinho Verde)

The Malvasia in the blend gives this wine a perfumed character. It lends the wine a nutty character balancing the wine’s soft acidity. Drink this attractive wine now. Organic. Editors’ Choice. 90 Points — R.V.

$12 Portugal Vineyards

Constantino Ramos 2024 Juca Loureiro (Vinho Verde)

There’s a bright intensity to this wine on the palate, as a beam of tart citrus and mineral undertones are filled out by aromatics of nectarine, coriander seed, and river stone. This will be just the thing for oysters and clams on the half shell or simply prepared sushi/crudo/ceviche. Ready to drink over the next 2-3 years. 90 Points — J.S.

$26 Divine Vintage

Casa de Vilacetinho 2022 Reserva Avesso (Vinho Verde)

A bright fruity wine with a floral aroma and a tangy, vibrant texture, this is full of pink grapefruit and spicy flavors. It comes from an estate established in 1790, a wine that is rich and full in the mouth. Drink from later this year. 91 Points — R.V.

$21 Portugal Vineyards

Anselmo Mendes 2024 Muros Antigos Alvarinho (Vinho Verde Monção e Melgaço)

The wine’s ripe citrus fruits are full and bursting with vitality. The intensity is enhanced by orange zest flavors. The wine has a kiwi and white peach aroma that emphasizes its ripeness. Drink from 2026. 91 Points — R.V.

$17 PJ Wine

Zé Inconformado Maceração Pelicular 2021 Colheita Loureiro (Vinho Verde)

The tangy and vivid character reflects the name of this wine. Honey and spice aroma with grassy and pineapple flavor is piqued by an almost toasty nutty background. It has density and a fine texture. Named for the Barca coop’s nonconformist CEO and winemaker José Oliveira, known as Zé. Drink now. 90 Points — R.V.

$30 Portugal Vineyards

title: How Can White Wine Be Made from Red Grapes?
summary:
Winemakers around the world are producing excellent bottlings of White Malbec, White Pinot Noir, and more.

The post How Can White Wine Be Made from Red Grapes? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Have you ever had the elusive and confusing experience of tasting a white wine made from a red grape, such as a White Malbec? I have poured wines like this for friends, and the shocked looks are just priceless. “Wait, isn’t that a red grape? Why is this wine clear?” 

The answer is surprisingly simple. Despite their dark skins, most red grapes contain nearly colorless juice, just the same as white (green) grapes. The color we associate with red wine doesn’t come from the juice—it comes from the skins.

When making a traditional red wine, the juice remains in contact with those skins during fermentation, extracting color, tannin, and structure. But if the grapes are pressed gently and the juice is separated immediately, virtually no color is extracted, if any at all. Without skin contact, the winemaker ends up with a virtually clear white wine made from red grapes.

If you have ever enjoyed a Blanc de Noirs Champagne, you have already experienced this phenomenon. Most Champagne producers have utilized this technique for centuries to create Champagnes by transforming Pinot Noir and Pinot Meunier into elegant white sparkling wines. 

Some of Champagne’s most respected houses, including Bollinger, Billecart-Salmon, and Ruinart, have built loyal followings around these distinctive bottlings. Today, winemakers around the world are taking the same approach with still wines, creating some of the wine world’s most intriguing conversation starters.

From White Malbec to White Pinot Noir

Argentina has emerged as the home of White Malbec, with producers such as Trivento and Bodegas Salentein showcasing a brighter and cleaner side of the country’s flagship grape. What’s the winemaking secret, you ask? Chief winemaker of Trivento Winery, Germán Di Cesare, shared his secret: “We pick the grapes extremely early, and add a bit of oxygen to help remove any color coming from the grape.”

These wines boast a combination of green apple and pear aromas, while also displaying red cherries and strawberries, which can be uncommon for still wines. Pair that up with soft tannins and the acidity of a white wine and you have a bottle that is unique and beautifully complex. 

In Oregon, White Pinot Noir and Cabernet Franc have both developed something of a cult following. Willamette Valley Vineyards is among the pioneers of White Pinot, with producers including Lange Estate Winery and Left Coast Estate continuing to craft compelling examples. These wines feature the soft red fruit and floral notes you would expect from Pinot, while the lighter pear, citrus, and white peach aromas and flavors give them just the right amount of lift.

White Cabernet Franc (also known as “Blanc Franc”) is also having a moment in Oregon, with producers such as Jackalope Wine Cellars and Leah Jørgensen Cellars leading the way. Leah Jorgensen is credited as the first in America to make still white wine from Cabernet Franc. She aptly points out that, “You wouldn’t make a white wine arbitrarily from just any red grape. Cab Franc has the needed acidity, like Pinot Noir and Pinot Meunier in Champagne.” 

These wines tend to have less of the red fruit note, but they still carry that vibrant acidity along with the classic peppery and tobacco notes inherent to the grape.

What to Expect in the Glass

Many of these wines are nearly crystal clear, with only the faintest hints of silver, pale straw, or green reflections. Unlike Chardonnay or Viognier, whose deeper colors can come from lees aging, oak maturation, or controlled oxidation, these wines retain their almost transparent appearance because they experience little oxidation and virtually no skin contact, if any.

What I really love about these “blanc de noirs” still wines is their texture. Since the skins are removed immediately, very few phenolic compounds and virtually no tannins are extracted. The result is a mouthfeel that is softer and smoother than their red counterparts, yet many retain more mid-palate weight than lighter white wines like Pinot Grigio or Sauvignon Blanc. Because of the process, they maintain their brightness and vibrancy without the richness and oak influence associated with a full-bodied white.

That versatility makes these wines incredibly food-friendly. They pair beautifully with raw shellfish, grilled fish, roast chicken, charcuterie boards, and dishes that might otherwise leave you debating whether to open a white or a lighter red. These wines spark conversations around the table and keep you and your guests coming back for a second (or maybe even a third!) glass. Oh, and did I mention they are perfect for summer?

I think that’s part of their charm—they can humble even the most experienced wine people. Just when we think we’ve learned all there is to know, a glass of White Malbec or Pinot Noir comes along and reminds us that there are still discoveries waiting around the next corner.

Bottles to Try

Artesano de Argento 2023 White Malbec (Agrelo)

Delicate notes of white flowers perfume the nose. On the palate echoes of those floral notes blend with hints of kiwi, adding a touch of tropical fruit. Light-bodied and refreshing, this white Malbec offers an unconventional expression of the variety with aromatic appeal. Best Buy. 89 Points — Jesica Vargas

$17 Ace Spirits

Open Claim 2023 White Pinot Noir (Mount Pisgah)

Made entirely with estate fruit that aged for 16 months in French oak, none of which was new, this white take on Pinot Noir is dazzling. The wine’s aromas alone are worth the price of admission, as golden raspberries and lemon curd are joined by notes of wet slate and white flowers. Slightly elevated acidity and a bit of tannic structure support flavors like lemon, brioche and toasted filberts. This isn’t a light and wispy-textured wine, as those flavors feel like they are putting down a double coat on the palate. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — Michael Alberty

$75 Open Claim Vineyards

Concannon Vineyard 2025 Cabernet Franc Blanc Cabernet Franc (Livermore Valley)

This selection is a guilty pleasure. Cabernet Franc has long been a popular planting in the Livermore Valley. But, who would have thought to have minimal skin contact with the juice; which is what creates the Blanc. The aromatics are fresh, lively lychee, papaya, honeydew melon, pineapple, yellow plum, and a dash of allspice on nose. The flavors on the palate are white nectarine, peach, cantaloupe, ripe mango, and clove, with a generous amount of acidity. 93 Points — Tonya Pitts

$35 Concannon Vineyard

title: Once Forgotten, Viognier Is Back in the Spotlight. Try These Top Bottles.
summary:
Winemakers in both Viognier’s traditional home and growing regions across the globe are increasingly producing elegant renditions.

The post Once Forgotten, Viognier Is Back in the Spotlight. Try These Top Bottles. appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Viognier is a white grape variety historically grown in France’s Rhône Valley. Full-bodied and aromatically intense, it is sometimes blended with other white Rhône varietals like Marsanne and Roussanne. 

Though Viognier (pronounced VEE-oh-nyay) has long been made into stand-alone varietal wines, it has garnered a reputation for creating wines that are considered overly rich and thick. But that’s no longer accurate. 

Winemakers in both Viognier’s traditional home and growing regions across the globe are increasingly flipping the script and “consistently producing elegant renditions,” says Anna-Christina Cabrales, Wine Enthusiast’s tasting director and resident Rhône reviewer. “Gone are the days where Viognier is just really fat and dense.”

These single-varietal bottles span a wide range of textures and aromas, making them ideal partners for boldly flavored fare like Indian cuisine, complex Thai dishes, and Filipino classics.

In hotter vintages, Viognier often takes on tropical ginger and ripe citrus flavors. In cooler growing seasons, these flavors have a leaner expression, with lemongrass and spices, like white pepper and saffron, joining the mix.

The best examples deliver ethereal, elegant qualities. “It doesn’t feel like you are chomping into a bowl of fruit cocktail Jell-O,” she says. “It’s like a ballerina that dances across the palate.”

No matter the year or the region, however, these wines warrant exploration. From Condrieu in the Rhône Valley to California to Australia’s Eden Valley, these Viognier wines deserve space on your buying list.

California

In the United States, Viognier had a big moment in the 1990s, thanks to hundreds of California wineries. It faded into relative obscurity—never quite becoming the “next big grape” it was predicted to be. However, wider appreciation for the varietal is growing, in part because of all the lovely Viogniers being produced in the Golden State. 

“Viognier is a beautiful aromatic grape varietal that has the opportunity to shine and thrive in California,” says Wine Enthusiast writer-at-large Tonya Pitts, who reviews wines from California. “The expressions produced can be ethereal, drenched in sunshine and a field of florals, or dense, tantalizing, and thought-provoking. There is an interpretation to satisfy various palates.”

According to the Oxford Companion, the state has the world’s second biggest area of Viognier planted: just over 2,555 acres in 2020, largely located in the Central Coast. California Viogniers tend to be higher in alcohol due to ripening under the state’s famous sun.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Miraflores Winery 2023 Estate Viognier (El Dorado)

Fresh aromas of white stone fruits, honeysuckle, and notes of beeswax with a subtle kiss of barrel say, “I am a new world expression of Viognier, but you know who I am.” There is no identity crisis, only the vision of the bottling through a longer hangtime on the vine for a lush mouthfeel with vibrant acidity. Pair with grilled diver scallops, fennel, herbed butter. 92 Points — Tonya Pitts

$32 Miraflores Winery

Cedarville Vineyard 2023 Estate Viognier (El Dorado)

The aromas are lush, with notes of citrus rind, Meyer lemon, Yellow apple, and honeysuckle, layered with honeycomb on the nose. The flavors are marzipan, White nectarine, youthful apple, and lime curd, laced with bright acidity, and a long, mouth-watering finish. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — T.P.

$33 Cedarville Vineyard

Jacuzzi 2024 Clarum White Blend (Petaluma Gap)

This amber-tinged, amphorae aged blend erupts from the glass with aromas of concentrated peach, lemongrass, Meyer lemon, and white flowers. The palate is decadent, with rich extraction, plush acidity, and flavors of green apple, lemon peel, jasmine, and tangerine thrumming through the finish. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — Tom R. Capo

$45 Jacuzzi Family Vineyards

Barossa Valley

Some of the most exciting Viognier expressions are currently coming out of Australia’s Barossa Valley—specifically, the cool climate Eden Valley. Resisting the varietal’s reputation for flabbiness, the region’s standout Viogniers are zippy, food-friendly, and exceptionally luscious.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Yalumba 2023 The Virgilius Viognier (Eden Valley)

An oh-so-Viognier perfume lures the senses with heady notes of honeysuckle, cherry blossom, a white peach, lemon curd and white pepper. One you manage to pry your nostrils from the glass to take a sip, you’re met with a fulsome, mouth-filling palate that’s creamy but never heavy or syrupy, those floral and fruit notes flowing more subtly to the finish. Pair with buttered chicken or a Thai curry. 94 Points — Christina Pickard

$57 Wine.com

Yalumba 2023 Samuel’s Collection Viognier (Eden Valley)

The calling card white variety for this historic Barossa estate, this is, in typical Viognier fashion, a full figured white wine, all curves and soft edges, with appealing aromas of white peach, jasmine flower and a lick of honey. Chill down well and sip beside mushroom risotto or sweet buttery lobster. 92 Points — C.P.

$20 Wine Exchange

Tim Smith 2023 Viognier (Eden Valley)

There’s lovely bright lemony freshness—with hints of dried herbs and florals—to start. These morph into flavors on the palate, combining with a chalky texture and a wonderfully zippy, bouncy acidity. Makes for a food friendly bottling that avoids the flabbiness for which this variety is prone. 91 Points — C.P.

$25 Viscount Wines

Rhône Valley

Viognier has been grown on the steep terraces of the Northern Rhône for centuries. Plantings declined through the mid-20th century due to low productivity, but the grape was launched into international stardom in the early 1990s with the rise in popularity of Condrieu, the yellow-hued French wine made solely from the grape.

While famous for Condrieu, Viognier is also well-suited to blending with other Rhône varieties, including Marsanne, Roussanne, Grenache Blanc, Clairette, and Picardan. Rhône’s white wines make up roughly 10% of the region’s production, “yet offer a remarkable range of styles from bright and zesty to rich and full-bodied,” says Cabrales.

“If people have not picked up a glass of Viognier recently, they should.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Saint Cosme 2022 Viognier (Condrieu)

A beautifully sophisticated meld of sweet florals and ripe orchard fruit. This wine bursts with overripe white peach, apricot, star fruit, Korean pear, geraniums, and acacia honey. The aromas are lush and some delicate, exuding an exacting refinement. The palate is linear with warm sweet baking spices leading the runway before giving room to pure and elegant orchard fruit that lingers endlessly. Freshly washed white mushroom and wet stone round out the finish, adding depth. Round yet elegant, with an exceptionally long finish—this wine is simply too pretty to put down. 95 Points — Anna-Christina Cabrales

$65 The Chicago Wine Company

Rémi Niero 2022 Chérytage Viognier (Condrieu)

Delicate and perfumed, this wine offers high-toned aromas of citrus zest, Korean pear, apricot, peach blossom and white peony. The palate balances fruit and sweet spice with a subtle floral lift, achieving a refined and harmonious expression. A versatile choice for complex, spice-driven dishes. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$155 Station Plaza Wine & Spirits

Vidal-Fleury 2019 Viognier (Condrieu)

This wine seamlessly blends fresh pear, white peach, ginger, acacia honey, and wet stone aromas. The palate opens with pithy citrus, coriander, and honeysuckle, evolving into honey and clove. Round and textured, its lively acidity maintains balance and lift. Enjoy now or cellar through 2030+ for added depth. 93 Points — A.C.C.

$82 Beverage Boss

title: The Real-Life Wines of ‘The Odyssey’—and Where to Find Them
summary:
As Christopher Nolan’s star-studded adaptation of ‘The Odyssey’ hits theaters, we’re celebrating the epic’s best wine moments.

The post The Real-Life Wines of ‘The Odyssey’—and Where to Find Them appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
As Christopher Nolan’s star-studded adaptation of The Odyssey storms cinemas worldwide, wine takes on a recurring role of its own.

Samuel Butler’s translation of Homer’s epic, which is free to read on Project Guttenburg, mentions wine 176 times. Even the sea, at once an obstacle and vehicle to Odysseus’ homecoming, is described in many translations as “wine-dark” or “wine-faced” in lieu of blue. 

Southern Mediterranean communities have long claimed The Odyssey, naming cities, mountains, and geological wonders after the tale’s most memorable personalities. Local lore infuses language and culture so profoundly that history and mythology begin to blur. While a Bronze-era warrior king likely existed—and archeological excavations at ancient Troy suggest multiple conquests—viticulture and a thriving wine trade are indisputable facts. Wine plays a central role throughout The Odyssey, symbolizing Greek tenets of civilization, religion, prosperity, home, and hospitality. 

The Odyssey’s people and places may be fiction, but the wine was real. So, what were they drinking? Raise a glass alongside Odysseus and company on this wine-soaked itinerary.

Odysseus Spares Maron, Priest of Apollo 

Geographic Location: Ismarus (Eastern Macedonia and Thrace)

Probable Wines: Assyrtiko, Malagousia, Limnio

Following the siege of Troy, Odysseus’ crew sack nearby Ismarus, where they meet Maron, a priest of Apollo. Odysseus spares him and his wife and child. In return, Maron gifts him “twelve jars of sweet wine, unblended, and of the most exquisite flavor.” 

Greek scholars describe Maron as a son or a grandson of Dionysius and as “keeper of the vines.” Maronia (Maroneia) is one of Greece’s most ancient wine-growing regions. 

Indigenous white grapes Assyrtiko and Malagousia thrive there, along with red Mavroudi and Limnio, one of the world’s oldest recorded varieties. Of note, Limnio gets a shout out in Homer’s Illiad, the precursor to The Odyssey, as troops stock up on “necessary supplies” before the siege of Troy. You can sip along to the epic with local Protected Geographical Indication (PGI) Ismaros, comprising the region referred to as Maronia and surrounding areas.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Achaia Cellars 2022 Coastal Assyrtiko (Peloponnese)

Lively citrus aromas and minerally notes burst out of the glass of this Assyrtiko from Greece’s Peloponnese. The palate is similarly bright and balanced, with pleasant salinity and bitterness plus a long, tart, food-friendly finish. Best Buy. 91 Points — Emily Saladino

Inquire with the importer

The Cyclops Polyphemus Gets Mad

Geographic Location: Aci Trezza

Probable Wines: Nerello Mascalese, Nerello Cappuccio, Carricante

At the foot of Sicily’s Mount Etna, north of the Catanian coast, the real town of Aci Trezza is believed to be the home of one of The Odyssey’s most famous characters: the one-eyed giant known as Cyclops Polyphemus. 

Aci Trezza’s unique landscape formed during underwater volcanic eruptions, which cooled lava into stacks and columns known as i faraglioni. But according to the legend, these geological marvels came into being when blinded, drunken Polyphemus launched boulders at Odysseus and his crew as they fled. 

Prior to this episode, Odysseus has a feeling that Maron’s wine will come in handy. As he tells it, “I filled a large skin with this wine […] that I might […] deal with some savage…” 

He recounts the discovery of Polyphemus’ cliffside cave filled with goat and sheep cheese, which the crew raids greedily. Justifiably, the monster traps and devours two of them for dinner and another two for breakfast. 

In an effort to placate the monster, Odysseus offers Polyphemus Maron’s undiluted wine, then blinds him and stages an escape. Before he’s attacked, the monster drunkenly delights, “We have wine even in this country, for our soil grows grapes and the sun ripens them, but this drinks like Nectar and Ambrosia all in one.”  

Mount Etna is littered with evidence of Greek viticulture. Today’s indigenous varieties—Nerello Mascalese and Nerello Cappuccio for the reds, and Carricante and Minella for the whites—do not appear specifically in ancient texts. 

That said, the bold fruit and smoky finish of Etna Rosso DOC pairs beautifully with salty pecorino.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Ghiaia Nera 2023 Nerello Mascalese (Etna)

The nose sparks with aromas of raspberry, wild strawberry, flint, pepper and bitter orange. On the palate, cranberries and tart forest berries prance down a backbone of chili pepper and earth. Tannins are textured but fluid—sand-like but not gritty, rather shifting and covering space on your palate to fill voids as they appear. 92 Points — Danielle Callegari

$27 Wine.com

Aeolus, Keeper of the Winds, Feasts for a Month

Geographic Location: Aeolus’s island (Aeolia), or The Aeolian Islands

Probable Wines: Malvasia, Carinto Nero

Safe from the Cyclops, Odysseus and his surviving shipmates land on the island of Aeolia. Frequently translated as “the Aeolian island,” the site is part of the Aeolian archipelago, off Sicily’s northeastern coast. 

The crew encounters Aeolus, a king entrusted by Zeus to rule the winds. Aeolus hosts them for a month of feasting “and enjoying every conceivable kind of luxury.” Aeolus’ six lusty sons marry his six daughters, but before everyone dashes off to bed, they consume copious roasted meats and wine. 

Presumably, Malvasia. 

Imported by the Greeks, Malvasia flourishes on volcanic terrain. Try the dessert version, Malvasia delle Lipari Denominazione di Origine Controllata (DOC), for lush aromas. Dry Malvasia from Salina Island smells like apricot nectar and finishes with a rush of seawater. The local red, Corinto Nero (Black Corinth), is rare and spicy—great with grilled meat and awkward feelings about ancient incest.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Cantina di Venosa 2024 Verbo Malvasia Bianca (Basilicata)

On the nose, white peach and Bosc pear are complemented by the floral levity of jasmine, lime and salt. The palate intensifies with peach slices in syrup, green apple candy and pear tart, but that salted lime element persists for balance, and receives a boost from dynamic acid. Best Buy. 90 Points — D.C.

$24 Pier Wines

Circe’s Enchantment 

Geographic Location: Monte Circeo

Probable Wines: Trebbiano, Bellone, Nero Buono di Cori

South of Rome, Monte Circeo looms above the coastline. It is believed to be the place where sorceress Circe blends a secret potion into wine, milk, grains, and honey to transform all but Odysseus into pigs. 

Named in her honor, Circeo DOC consists mainly of Trebbiano. Local white grape Bellone blooms with bright citrus, wildflowers, and sea breezes. Red variety Nero Buono di Cori is another one of the region’s most ancient varieties, bursting with cherry and violet notes. 

None of these wines will turn you into a pig, but moderation is a virtue. Just ask the Greeks.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Suavia 2020 Massifitti Trebbiano (Veronese)

Hints of roasted pear and smoky almond lead on the nose, with a pop of white pepper. Crunchy green apple and a touch of flint drive the palate. Nuanced and layered with waves of orchard and stone fruits cascading into rocks crushing them and then lifting them with each successive wave. A complex wine that will only continue to evolve with time. Drink Now – 2035. Cellar Selection. 94 Points — Jeff Porter

$30 Toast Wines by Taste

The Sirens Beckon

Geographic Location: Li Galli Islands

Probable Wines: Falanghina, Freco, Piedirosso, Aglianico

The Li Galli archipelago, visible from Positano along the Amalfi Coast, has been known since ancient times as Le Sirenuse. An italianization of the Latin, Sirenusae: home of the sirens. 

On Circe’s advice, Odysseus orders his crew to tie him to the ship’s mast. They all stuff their ears with wax and manage to resist the sirens’ alluring song. 

Had they docked for a feast, there’s a good chance they would have enjoyed some of Italy’s oldest indigenous varieties, including (but not limited to): white Falanghina, Biancolella, Forastera, and Greco, and red Piedirosso, Sciascinoso, and Aglianico.

Mount Vesuvius towers over this volcanic terroir, lending mineral verve and longevity to everything wine that grows. The winemakers on these extreme, terraced vineyards along the coast are heroes. Celebrate them with a Costa d’Amalfi DOC.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

La Guardiense 2024 Alfabeto – Janare Falanghina (Falanghina del Sannio)

The nose has the white flower and lemon scent of fresh laundry. That same element defines the palate, but notes of lime and honey add nuance and create a mouthfeel which is not only chalky, textured and substantial, but also fresh and citrusy. 92 Points — D.C.

$14 Saratoga Wine

Odysseus Returns Home

Geographic Location: Ithaca

Probable Wines: Robola of Kefalonia 

The isle of Ithaca bookends The Odyssey. While awaiting Odysseus’ return, his wife and son Penelope and Telemachus fend off suitors who have stormed their palace and feast there nightly. 

Our hero returns after twenty years—ten at war, and ten at sea. He joyously reunites with his wife, son, and father, Laertes, over a pig roast, and presumably copious cups of mineral-driven, bright and citrusy Robola—an ancient indigenous variety not to be confused with Italian Ribolla Gialla or Slovenian Rebula. 

Ithaca is part of the Ionian archipelago, where wine production is centered on neighboring island, Kefalonia. 

Pour one out for the love of faithful family with Protected Designation of Origin (PDO) Robola of Kefalonia.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Sclavos 2019 Di Sasso Robola (Cephalonia)

This intense white starts with aromas of white flowers, tangerine, peach and grapefruit, followed by vibrant flavors of lemon, peach, stone and orange rind. Medium bodied with bright acidity, the wine finishes with toasted nut and honey. 90 Points — Saki Kimura

$28 D.Vino Wine and Spirits

title: 8 Burger and Wine Pairings to Upgrade Your Cookout
summary:
No matter what type of burger you're cooking up, consider this your sign to break out the wine glasses.

The post 8 Burger and Wine Pairings to Upgrade Your Cookout appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Break out the sunscreen—outdoor drinking season is in full swing. Whether it’s radlers on the beach or IPAs in the backyard, there’s nothing quite like the pairing of a juicy, fresh-off-the-grill burger and ice cold beer on a hot summer day. But burgers and wine can be equally harmonious together. Burgers are casual, fun, and comforting, so they’re best complemented by wines that share those traits.

Paula de Pano, sommelier and owner of Rocks + Acid, a wine shop and tasting room in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, wants her burger-and-wine experience to feel almost primal.

“I’m not looking for a revelatory moment when I choose to drink wine with a burger,” de Pano says. “I want it to be an everyman kind of experience, no-frills and gut-satisfying.”

Meanwhile, when Bambi Ray, assistant general manager and wine & spirits buyer at Apero restaurant in Monroe, Louisiana, thinks about burgers on the grill, she’s drawn to bright, uplifting wines.

“You don’t want something that’s going to feel super heavy and weigh you down,” Ray explains. “You want something refreshing—nothing that makes you want to take a nap.”

No matter what type of burger you’re cooking up, consider this your sign to break out the wine glasses. (Plastic is fine—after all, this is casual!) These burger and wine pairing picks, dreamed up by drinks experts and matched with bottles from the Wine Enthusiast Tasting Department, are sure to keep the party going all summer long.

Classic Hamburger: Piedmont Dolcetto

“A well-executed burger has incredible umami, which makes it versatile to pair with [many] styles of wines,” says Yannick Benjamin, Wine Enthusiast’s 2021 Sommelier and Beverage Director of the Year. He suggests “a beautiful Dolcetto from Piedmont.” 

Dolcetto means “little sweet one” in Italian, and although it isn’t sweet, its notes of juicy berry fruit and hint of savory heft helps it stand up to the quintessential beef burger. These wines are meant to be enjoyed young. “They have enough tannin to balance richer foods,” says Benjamin, but they won’t weigh you down.

Pecchenino 2023 San Luigi Dolcetto (Dogliani)

Here’s Dolcetto flexing all its muscles. Dark purple fruits hit first, then a crack of black pepper wakes up your senses. Violets drift above it all, adding complexity without getting precious. The structure means business but doesn’t forget Dolcetto’s friendly side. This is the wine showing you why it deserves more attention, serious enough for the wine geeks but fun enough for everyone else. Best Buy. 93 Points — Jeff Porter

$21 67 Wine

Turkey Burger: Full-Bodied Rosé

Ray recommends a fuller-bodied rosé to enjoy with a turkey burger, as there are often a lot of flavors contained in a single burger, from toppings and sauces to the patty itself. Because turkey patties are a little milder than their beefy cousins, they’re arguably more likely to be tricked out with condiments.

“Rosé is something that won’t be at odds with the flavors of lettuce, tomato, and avocado, and really pairs well with all the options,” Ray advises. She suggests opting for a heartier style of rosé with citrus and plum notes—it’s still refreshing enough to sip on a sunny afternoon, turkey burger in hand.

Domaines Ott 2024 Château Romassan Rosé (Bandol)

Intense aromas of peach and the ocean, this rosé is bright with fruit but also rich with minerality. The palate is immediately spicy and robust. Tons of minerality with great texture and a lingering heat. Pair with Bouillabaisse. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — Cody Wexler

$60 Wine.com

Cheeseburger: Beaujolais

Burger plus melted cheese equals a weighty flavor bomb, so you’re going to want “a bit more of a palate-cleansing wine, because the cheese brings an element of richness,” says Jahdea Gildin, sommelier at New York City’s Luthun. A lighter and brighter style of red wine that will lift you back up, like a Beaujolais, is a great option. It’s “nice and fresh, with red and black fruits, and a little bit of a floral note,” Gildin says. It’s a perfect foil for a meaty, cheesy cheeseburger.

Mont Bessay 2022 En Bessay Gamay (Juliénas)

Newcomer Domaine Mont Bessay continues to impress with every release. Lifted aromas of black cherry, briary blackberry and black olive roll around on the nose with layered hints of baking spice. The palate is fulsome on the attack yet reserved on the predicate with a supple texture. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — Reggie Solomon

$52 Wine Express

Lamb Burger: Areni Noir

Jill Weber, owner of multiple restaurants in Philadelphia—Jet Wine Bar, Rex, and Sor Ynez—suggests a wine with a complex nose, vivid fruit flavors, and an herbal undertone to help stand up to the slight funk of lamb. She opts for an Areni Noir from Armenia when possible. “Especially if the burger has some feta cheese, which they typically do. The bright cherry and black pepper of an Areni Noir red is a perfect pairing,” she notes.

Yacoubian-Hobbs 2022 Sarpina Areni (Vayots Dzor)

Rich and suave, with layers of blueberry parfait, damson, and a hint of cocoa. The tannins are melting and refined, giving the wine a seamless texture. Long and expressive on the finish, showing both depth and finesse. This is for the fans of the polished style. 91 Points — Aleks Zecevic

$45 Wines of Armenia

Traditional Veggie Burger: Lambrusco di Sorbara

When it comes to old-school veggie burgers, the kind where you can see black beans and corn right in the patty, “there’s still heft and richness, even though it’s vegetarian, so I don’t want anything to add too much weight,” says Ray.

She goes for Lambrusco di Sorbara, which hails from Italy’s Emilia-Romagna region. “It’s not super dark and inky like some Lambrusco,” Ray explains. “It drinks more like a full-bodied rosé or a light-bodied red, with good acidity and refreshing lift.”

Gavioli NV Sparkling Blend (Lambrusco di Sorbara)

Vibrant wild strawberry and red currants mingle with fresh green herbs and wild mint. This Sorbara shows characteristic zippy acidity balanced by lush, ripe forest berries. The palate stays light and energetic while delivering layers of untamed fruit and subtle herbaceous notes. 90 Points — J.P.

$16 Liquor Express

Bison Burger: Gamay

A bison burger is quite lean, so choose a light, slightly fizzy, and zippy wine that will complement, rather than overpower it. De Pano recommends a super drinkable Gamay, “with a light fizz that interplays well with bison’s leanness.” 

She says sipping the wine is “almost like taking a joyride in a ‘50s Thunderbird—electric and fun with sour cherry, crunchy red delicious apples, coriander, and citrus peel.”

Evening Land 2024 Seven Springs Gamay Noir (Eola-Amity Hills)

Light and elegant, this Gamay Noir soars on gossamer wings. It is also the best example of Gamay Noir from Evening Land that I have ever tasted. Fresh, clean aromas of cranberry, orange zest and a chest of cedar drawers are flanked by a trace of bittersweet dark chocolate. Crisp acidity then does its thing for the wine’s dark raspberry, black pepper and white tea flavors. The wine feels tart and juicy in the mouth, with velvety tannins that possess a subtle grip. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — Michael Alberty

$45 Evening Land Wines

Seafood-Based Burger: Assyrtiko

The Assyrtiko grape is one of the most celebrated native Greek varietals. It originated in Santorini, but is now grown all over Greece and can even be found in areas like California. Sipping this crisp white with a tuna or salmon burger brings out the citrus, saline flavors of the wine while complementing the relatively delicate flavors of the fish.

“Assyrtiko has a nice, full mouthfeel, acidic freshness, and ample fruit,” says Weber. But it’s the “sea kiss” of the wine that makes it a spot-on choice for seafood. Tuna and salmon burgers are often dressed up with bold additions like Sriracha or ginger, and Assyrtiko plays nicely with these zingy, spicy flavors, too.

Perlegos 2024 Thera Block Assyrtiko (Clements Hills)

This is super exciting to see Greek varietals grown in California. While it might not be Santorini, the quality of fruit and flavor profile are spot on. Aromatics of zesty, saline enhanced citrus, stone fruits, and savory herbs are wafting across the nose; the flavors on the palate are youthful, lean Meyer lemon, marut, dried oregano and mineral scattered throughout the mouthfeel. Leaving you with mouthwatering acidity and dynamic fruit to finish. 93 Points — Tonya Pitts

$23 Bottle Barn

Impossible or Beyond Meat Burger: Washington State Sparkling Wines

Gildin was a vegetarian for 21 years, and although he eats burgers on occasion now, he understands the longing for a non-meat—yet meaty—experience.

“Impossible burgers are new, so it makes sense to choose a wine that is also new,” he says. Gildin suggests a wine that has “bright freshness and white fruit” to “cut through the richness of faux meat.” Give Washington sparkling wines a shot here, with their light-bodied nature and up-and-coming energy.

With burgers, don’t be afraid to experiment and play. Chances are, you’ll surprise yourself with something satisfying and tasty.

Capital Call 2023 Blanc de Blancs Chardonnay (Columbia Valley)

It is hard to miss the Granny Smith Green apple aroma emitted by Capital Call’s Blanc de Blancs. That tangy introduction is followed by notes of guava, saline and brioche. The wine’s flavors of grapefruit and lemon zest fell tart and tangy on the palate, with zippy acidity dueling with frisky bubbles to see who will command more attention. 92 Points — M.A.

$50 Capitol Call Vinters

title: North America’s ‘Best Sommelier’ Proves That Accessibility Is the Future of Wine
summary:
The wine director at New York City’s Le Bernardin and owner of Aldo Sohm Wine Bar wants to make wine more approachable for all.

The post North America’s ‘Best Sommelier’ Proves That Accessibility Is the Future of Wine appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
One of the most enthusiastic receptions for any winner at the North America’s 50 Best Restaurants ceremony in New Orleans on May 28 was for Aldo Sohm—North America’s Best Sommelier 2026. As the wine director at New York City’s three-Michelin-starred Le Bernardin, and the owner of its Midtown neighbor Aldo Sohm Wine Bar, he balances expertise and technical knowledge with joy and accessibility (his two books, after all, are titled Wine Simple and Wine Simple: Perfect Pairings). At a time when awards tend to favor the shiny and new, this recognition speaks to his lasting influence, curiosity, and evangelism for an industry that’s constantly being reshaped and challenged. But how does it feel to be a region’s “best” sommelier in 2026?

“The wine world was never as exciting as it is now, so that’s the fun part,” he says. “Lobster and Chardonnay is great, I can serve it no problem, but it’s boring. People think we serve mostly Burgundy, but I’m just as passionate about Canary Islands’ wines. Wine keeps you humble and hungry to learn more.”

That sense of discovery has shaped everything from his bestselling books to his wine bar, which he opened to make great wine feel less intimidating. “I wanted to break the formality a little bit, like you’re coming to my living room,” he says. “Everybody expected that I’d serve $30-and-north glasses, but that’s when you make wine inaccessible. We’re pouring the ‘Breisgau‘ from Bernhard Huber, a German Pinot Blanc and Pinot Gris blend that tastes almost like Bourgogne Blanc, but it’s a fraction of the price.”

Pairings Should Elevate, Not Dominate

Sohm is a passionate advocate for how the right food pairings let a wine express its full personality and potential. He remembers discovering the vinos de pasto (unfortified dry whites from Andalucía) of Willy Pérez and Ramiro Ibáñez and immediately thinking about what dishes they could unlock; recently he’s served them alongside uni with dashi custard. “These wines zigzag like crazy, you worry it might just be for the wine freaks, but it’s like how we behave slightly differently when we’re single than when we’re married,” he says. “A great food and wine pairing is like a marriage where two people elevate each other without one dominating the other.”

Good Service Is About Emotion

As I talked with both judges and winners at the North America’s 50 Best Restaurants celebrations, the word that kept coming up was service. Restaurant lists can feel like checklists, but the truly great restaurants deliver emotion, not just performative theater. That’s where Sohm comes in.

“Of course people come for the food, but I think at the end of the day, you’re buying yourself time and it’s an experience,” says Sohm. “Nothing feels greater than being taken care of. But people want to be treated as humans, not ATM machines. Wine aside, even if I just pour your water and serve you dessert, I’m just as happy. This is what we call hospitality.” 

A mentor to many, Sohm says he’s continually inspired by industry colleagues. “Oddly enough, one of my best experiences recently was the night before the 50 Best awards because I’d never been to New Orleans,” he says. “We went to Emeril’s, where they cooked a storm, and their new wine director Aaron Benjamin had incredible wine pairings, super thoughtful and skilled, beautifully done and charming.”

He also points to Vanya Filipovic at Mon Lapin in Montreal, Chase Sinzer of Claud and Penny in New York, Pascaline Lepeltier of Chambers, and Bobby Stuckey of Colorado’s Frasca Hospitality Group. “When I have a day where it’s a little rough, when I question things, whose Instagram I look at is Bobby Stuckey. In terms of consistency, hospitality, even his personal lifestyle, he inspires me.”

Whether sharing a meal with friends or winding down after work, this refreshing style of wine makes even

The post Luminous Bubbles with Mediterranean Spirit: Introducing Esprit de Cote des Roses appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Luminous Bubbles with Mediterranean Spirit: Introducing Esprit de Cote des Roses

Sparkling or Rosé, Why Choose? This Dazzling French Bottle Does It All

Popping the cork on a bottle of sparkling rosé is always a good idea. Whether sharing a meal with friends or winding down after work, this refreshing style of wine makes even the smallest moments feel special. One of France’s most iconic producers just released a brand-new expression. Esprit de Cote des Roses, the latest creation from the team at Gérard Bertrand is crafted in the sun-drenched south of France. This captivating sparkling meshes Mediterranean spirit with timeless savoir-faire, all wrapped in an eye-catching bottle that’s certain to leave an impression.  

For over 10 years, Gérard Bertrand’s Cote des Roses still rosé has captured the hearts of industry professionals and wine lovers alike, thanks to its juicy fruit-forwardness and palate-cleansing acidity. After establishing its status as a benchmark reference for French rosé, the visionary brand sought to create its first-ever sparkling edition of the wine, with the estate’s next generation, Emma Bertrand, at the helm of the project.

Designed to elevate the everyday, this enchanting bottle of sparkling rosé delivers both on quality and taste, with its striking, rose-imprinted bottle as the finishing touch— conceptualized by none other than Emma Bertrand herself. Esprit de Cotes des Roses is born from a blend of Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, two of France’s most classic grapes for sparkling wine production, along with the addition of local Grenache, which adds a unique Mediterranean flair to the traditional assemblage. 

Just the heart of the press is used in the juice, promising purity and finesse in each estate-produced bottle. A small amount of reserve wine is blended into the mix, bringing depth and complexity with brioche-driven undertones. After resting in barrel for several months, the wine undergoes a secondary fermentation in bottle before aging sur-lie and bottling with a small amount of dosage. A pale pink hue, the wine reveals subtle aromas of freshly cut red berries and white flower petals, leading to a lively and joyful palate marked by a delicate, creamy mousse and a long and refreshing finish—one distinctly attributed to the cool, high-altitude Pyrenean hills where the fruit is carefully grown.

Moreover, Esprit de Cote des Roses’ structured-meets-easygoing profile makes it perfect for enjoying across an array of jovial moments. The wine’s innate fruit-forwardness and high levels of acidity make it ideal for pairing with an array of dishes, from crisp, seasonal salads and cheese boards to zesty ceviches, salty potato chips, fried appetizers, and spicy curries alike.

Despite its immense food-friendliness, Esprit de Cote des Roses can equally stand on its own, making it just as reliable of a choice for drinking solo as it does for pairing with food. 

Above all, Esprit de Cote des Roses captures the essence of the sun-drenched south of France, where cool mountain air meets the salty Mediterranean Sea. It’s a wine designed to inspire convivial memories, seamlessly turning the ordinary to the exceptional with the simple pop of a cork. 

To learn more about Esprit de Cote des Roses, visit https://www.gerard-bertrand.com/en or follow @thecotedesroses on Instagram.

title: The Best Wines for Every Regional Style of American Barbecue
summary:
From Carolina whole hog to Texas brisket, every type of barbecue dish deserves a great wine pairing.

The post The Best Wines for Every Regional Style of American Barbecue appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Beer may be the typical beverage of choice for America’s big barbecue holidays, but in-the-know drinkers are well aware that an aptly paired wine can take grilled or smoked meat to the next level.

“There are so many great ways to work wine into really any type of cuisine, but particularly with barbecue because there are so many cultural similarities between the way that wine gets made and enjoyed and the way that barbecue gets made and enjoyed,” says Matthew Brown, a wine educator and consultant in Virginia.

Both wine and barbecue take time and skill to make, boast subtle flavor nuances, and highlight regional variation.

Some meats are spice-rubbed. Some are smoked. Some come covered with a sauce, while others boast crunchy, flavorful bark. But regardless of the cut of meat or how it’s made, “you’ll want a wine that can carry you through the whole meal,” says Dana Beninati, a food and wine educator in New York City and the host of Dine With Dana.

Barbecue and Wine Pairing 101

There are some key characteristics worth looking for in a bottle regardless of what specific type of American barbecue you’re enjoying. Start with wines that are refreshing, skew on the lighter side, and are well-balanced.

“I’m looking for [wines with] great acidity and low to medium alcohol but also intensity of flavor that is thirst-quenching,” Brown says. “Because to me, as a Southern boy, I think the quintessential barbecue drink is sweet tea. So, I want my wine to have that same light, refreshing, easy-to-drink quality—but also bold flavors.”

Beninati agrees that fresh, light, and flavorful are the common themes to seek out, and a significant indicator of those qualities in wine is high acidity. “Richness and fattiness play a huge part in barbecue, and we all know how we feel after we eat a really rich, heavy meal,” Beninati says. “You need something refreshing to create balance for the richness of the barbecue.”

Abe Zarate, wine director at Crane Club in New York City, suggests choosing something chilled, like a lighter-style red wine, white, or rosé. “Chillable reds are a big thing right now,” Zarate says. “But I would argue any red could be chillable if that enhances the experience. It’s more about digging deeper into what you’re consuming.”

Sparkling wine is also a great pairing partner for most famous regional barbecues on menus. “The undertones that every barbecue pit master plays around with—smoke, spice, caramelization, fire, browning—work so well with sparkling wine,” Beninati says. “So, it’s a very safe option.”

Below, Beninati, Brown, and Zarate share their favorite pairing partners for some of the most popular styles of U.S. barbecue.

Eastern Carolina Whole Hog with Vinegar Sauce: Cava

Down in the eastern side of Tar Heel State, whole hogs are smoked over a pit. When the meat is done, it’s chopped up, with the white and dark parts mixed together, then soaked in a tangy vinegar sauce. The thin and sharp sauce—which highlights the smoky and savory notes of the pork—is similar to the vinegar-based Escabeche that’s commonly found on top of grilled meats and fish in Spain, says Benanti.

This is why she suggests pairing it with a Spanish Cava. “It’s so elegant but still an easy sipper that can balance out the flavors of barbecue,” she says. “And the price point isn’t aggressive. I am blown away by the quality when we’re talking value for money.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Juvé y Camps 2021 Reserva de la Familia Xarel·lo-Parellada-Macabeo (Cava)

Lifted aromatics carry into the palate on a silken mousse and vibrant acidity. Citrus blossom, passionfruit, and crushed almonds gain freshness with a sprinkle of sea salt and a long finish. 91 Points — Elaine Chukan Brown

$23 Wine.com

Carolina Gold Pulled Pork: Vouvray

When looking for barbecue in South Carolina, the glorious mustardy Carolina Gold sauce is usually stacked high on plates. Pulled pork is covered in a mustard-based sauce mixed with vinegar, brown sugar, pepper, and spice that offers a tangy and spicy flavor and hints of sweetness.

Search for a slightly off-dry Chenin Blanc, like those made in France’s Loire Valley, to play up the sweetness of the sauce. “Wouldn’t it be fun to have something with the right amount of acid but also a little touch sweet?,” says Brown. “Because you’re going to get a lot of acid with this wine, which I think you need when there’s that much intensity of flavor in the sauce.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Domaine Bourillon d’Orleans 2023 La Coulée d’Argent Chenin Blanc (Vouvray)

This delicate wine opens with enticing aromas of white tea, dried field grass and honeyed golden apple accented by orange blossom. The layered palate looks out into a late-summer meadow and frolics on a blanket of orchard fruit that offers savory hints that are complemented by a dollop of orange marmalade on toast. Deliciously attractive. 93 Points — Reggie Solomon

$22 Elk Wine

Alabama Chicken with White Sauce: Petit Manseng

It’s all about the white sauce in Alabama, particularly in the bustling city of Birmingham. A creamy and tangy condiment, primarily made with mayonnaise, vinegar, and pepper, the sauce provides a rich flavor that accentuates the smokiness of the chicken. It’s a perfect match for a textural Petit Manseng. “You need something with texture and body that’s not necessarily full but has that backbone of structural acid that can reset the palate and prepare you for another bite of that rich, smoky chicken,” Zarate says.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Clos Regain 2023 La Côte des Brumes Petit Manseng (Jurançon Sec)

A rich, full wine from a single vineyard, this has a fine balance between dryness and touches of honey. Made from Petit Manseng, the wine is rich and dense, delicious to drink now. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — Roger Voss

Inquire with the importer

Memphis Dry Ribs: Carbonic Red Blend

One of the most known and popular styles of American barbecue is Memphis dry ribs coated in a blend of spices including paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, black pepper, and sometimes a bit of sugar. It’s an intense bomb of flavor for a sturdy cut of meat that deserves a wine that’s equally flavorful but also refreshing. A carbonic macerated red blend does the trick, according to Brown. “When I think of Memphis barbecue, there’s a little tang,” she says. “And so I like to match that intensity of flavor with a tart freshness and a smoky element.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Itä 2024 Carbonic Zinfandel (Walla Walla Valley (WA))

This unicorn wine undergoes one week of whole-cluster carbonic maceration and two days of post-foot-stomping skin contact. Fermentation and malolactic fermentation are completed in neutral French oak. The Zinfandel’s aromas of lavender and a blueberry muffin with a touch of lemon icing are hunger-pang-inducing. A laser beam of acidity guides lights up the wine’s strawberry and hibiscus flower tea flavors. What a tangy treat! 92 Points — Michael Alberty

$22 Ita Winery

Kansas City Burnt Ends: Sparkling Rosé

There’s a lovely combination of smoky, sweet, and savory flavors in every little piece of Kansas City burnt ends, a staple of Missouri barbecue. It’s made from the marbled tip of smoked brisket, which is coated in a sweet liquid blend of vinegar- and tomato-based sauces. “Let’s pair this with a lower-alcohol option that delivers on major flavors,” Beninati says, noting that the high acidity and effervescence of Pinot Noir-based sparkling rosé acts like a palate cleanser to prep for the next bite.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Ultraviolet NV Sparkling Rosé Pinot Noir (California)

Tiny fine bubbles swirl throughout bright aromas of raspberry, Bing cherry, cotton candy, and florals on the nose, with a mid-palate filled with citrus zest, tart red apple, and skins on the finish. Best Buy. 91 Points — Tonya Pitts

$22 Ultraviolet Wines

St. Louis Ribs with Tomato Sauce: Franciacorta

It’s all about the sweet, tomato-y sauces slathered on baby back ribs in St. Louis, Missouri. These ribs are known for their tender meatiness and are smothered in a sweet, tangy, and slightly smoky sauce that enhances the natural taste of the pork. Beninati reaches for a glass of Franciacorta, Italy’s answer to Champagne. “I’m out in the sunshine; I’ve got my sunglasses on; I’ve got sauce on my face,” she says. “I’m reaching for a glass of this sparkling because it has so much structure to stand up to the rich boldness of this meat.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Fratelli Berlucchi NV Brut 25 Chardonnay (Franciacorta)

This steely blanc de blanc lifts out of the glass with crisp orchard fruit, fresh jasmine and lemon zest aromas giving way to subtle hints of brioche. The palate shows green apple, mixed citrus and hints of pineapple, finishing with a mineral edge. 91 Points — Jeff Porter

$52 Wine-searcher

Central Texas Brisket: Italian Cabernet Sauvignon

When it comes to barbecue, brisket is king. And nowhere does it quite like Central Texas. With a simple seasoning blend of black pepper, salt, and a pinch of cayenne, the complex flavors come from the low and slow cooking process—usually about 12 hours indirectly smoked with post oak. The final result is a meat with a juicy center enveloped in a dark, crunchy, spiced bark.

It needs a sturdy wine that can “complement every bite in any cut of the brisket: lean, fatty, or both,” like a Cabernet Sauvignon-heavy red blend from Bolgheri, Zarate says. “Grapes grown in this area are influenced by the coastal climate to the west and a natural mountainous amphitheater surrounding it,” he adds. “The breezier microclimate translates to a bold wine that offers ripe dark fruit with a backbone of fresh acidity.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Grattamacco 2023 Rosso Red Blend (Bolgheri)

The nose is earthy and umami, with aromas of damp soil, herbs, coffee, pencil shavings and blackberries. Sweet and tart blackberries are layered with herbs on the palate, while the finish is hot and peppery. Tannins are magnificently unapologetic, and there’s a sense of anticipation around what the wine will become when they begin to relax. 96 Points — Danielle Callegari

$32 Station Plaza

Santa Maria Tri-Tip: Cabernet Franc

On the West Coast, barbecue lovers revel in Santa Maria tri-tip, a hunk of beef that’s prepared with a basic blend of salt, pepper, and garlic salt. It’s grilled on a grate that gets lowered and raised above red-oak embers, which gives a crisp crust, juicy center, and smoky flavor. Brown recommends pairing it with a wine with a little “smoky, spicy kind of element,” like a Cabernet Franc. “It’s not overripe, it’s not big, it’s not jammy,” says Brown. “It’s really well-structured. It has beautiful tannins, and you could serve it at about 60 degrees or so. I think it shows well with just a little bit of chill on it.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Luna Hart 2023 Honeybear Orchard Vineyard Cabernet Franc (Los Olivos District)

The dense and dark core of berry and plum is cut by a fiercely spicy streak of cracked pepper on the exciting nose of this bottling. Snappy flashes of plum and elderberry pop on the sip, where that pepper adds both brightness and complexity to the tense, lingering mouthfeel. 95 Points — Matt Kettmann

$41 Luna Hart
